title: 2025/06/23 - TI
displayTitle: 2025-06-23 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Public Works (Critical Infrastructure) Amendment Bill
description: Monday 23 June 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Public Works (Critical Infrastructure) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: Māori Women's Welfare League (Dr Hope Tupara, National President) Sharon Campbell Environment and Conservation Organisations of NZ Aotearoa (Cath Wallace, Vice Chairperson) Transpower New Zealand Limited (Matt Fanning, Head of Land Owner Relations and Property; Raewyn Moss, Executive General Manager, Customers & External Affairs) For other hearings on this bill see parts 3 and 5 of 2025/06/19
descriptionSummary: Public Works (Critical Infrastructure) Amendment Bill. Māori Women's Welfare League (Dr Hope Tupara, National President). Sharon Campbell

chunks.text: You, uh, just briefly at the end as well, if you've got about two minutes, right? Good afternoon, Dr. Nice to have you here. Can you hear us? We've got, you are on mute though.

Can't hear you. Can you, can you get your mute button? Okay. Brilliant. Okay, great. Look, thank you very much. Um, welcome to this, uh, zoom submission, uh, process. We've got you down for 10 minutes. Uh, we've got some, uh, members on Zoom, some members in the room. Uh, and, um, I'll give you a call at about three minutes to go if you're still speaking, um, just to say we, we always like to ask questions, get a bit of to and fro going.

So, um, time is in your mor uh, afternoon johana as well. The time is in your hands. Uh, thank you for this time. I'm just gonna take my submission as read. Yes. Um, firstly, I wanna say that the league, we are not opposed to progress and we also are not, not opposed to owners making land deals with the government as long as the balance of power is equal and fairness is weighted equally for all parties.

So, um, I've, um, read the document must have been commissioned by the government by Minter Allison Redwood, um, which notes the significant public implications and quote notably the trade off between new compensation rights with weaker appeal options in the case of critical infrastructure acquisitions, uh, the leagues just opposed to attempts to railroad people who do not have the resources to fight the government, where the balance of power is tipped in favor of the government's agenda.

Uh, from my reading, um, I understand that landowners who object to the removal of their land being taken will need, need to appeal to the minister or local authority instead of the environment court. So the authority that is approving the acquisition is also the same authority that the landowner has to go to for an appeal of the acquisition, which really seems, well it is unfair, uh, the balance of power sits with the minister or the local authority.

Um, my submission is quite short. Um, our recommendation is that this bill goes back to the drawing board to put the balance of power equally for all of those. So there's a living level playing field, particularly for the owners. Um, the rapport from Minter, um, Alison Red Watts also makes mention of a subsequent bill that's coming.

So, um, it, I don't know what that bill is. Um, well just for those of you who don't know, eco, um, we were established around about 71, 72, 19 71, 72, and we have, uh, 14 member organizations and or thereabouts.

Um, and I guess we are very concerned at the way in which the, this bill, um, seems to want to sacrifice both private and public, um, interests for the sake of a very narrowly confined or, uh, defined set of projects for the sake of economic growth.

And we feel that this, um, sort of tries to be generous in the sense, and it's good that the, the idea is to give a bit of a margin, um, and incentivize people to, um, relinquish land. But when really unhappy about the way in which people, um, are given such a short time to decide whether they'll, uh, you know, make a submission 10 days.

Um, very often people are just not there and, and won't even know that they've been asked. Um, it also seems to us that the whole design of it is imagining that there's somebody sitting looking at their computer and ready to go immediately. Um, it doesn't allow four people's personal circumstances for the fact that they're in the middle of, um, lambing or fixing fences or whatever else it might be.

And, um, it, it's just not reasonable to give people such a short time to say that they want to participate and then a, a further short time of 20 working days or whatever it is, um, and really compressed timeframes. And yet the, the applicants for the project or the Crown and the case of the, um, roads of national significance, they've had ages to decide that they want a project and to design it and think about it and taking somebody's land is a really serious thing to do.

Um, and I don't think people will feel at all cooperative if they're given such a short timeframe. I think these short timeframes are likely to simply get people's backs up with mine anyway. Um, and, you know, land is a hugely more significant than its market value for many people.

Um, it, it has ecological and hydrological value. It has, um, all sorts of cultural and personal and social associations. So I think there should be much more requirement to consider alternatives, alternative route, or in the case particularly of Rons and, and some of those, um, fast track projects.

Well, do we really need to do it? Um, so that's a sort of strategic assessment. Um, what are the alternatives? Why should we have to just go in and ignore the options, for instance, of better public transport, um, and of better, um, use of the capital for other things.

Um, we do appreciate that sometimes you do the Crown needs to take land, but we don't think this is fair and we don't think there's sufficient recognition of to treat it or of, um, the, the values that are not market values. Um, we, um, I'm an economist and I know that there's a great deal of value that is, um, attached to land that has absolutely no market expression.

Um, and it just seems that the government is wanting to go so fast that people are not given a fair chance. We totally object to the idea that, um, the environment court is not to be available to people. I'm thinking of for instance, Patricia Grace and the hard work she did to try and hang on to the last little bit of their family land, um, where everything else had been taken.

Um, and we think it's completely unfair to have a system where the minister who is essentially, um, part of a government that has got this, um, misplaced and narrow-minded pursuit of economic growth, um, should be the decision maker. Um, and, and that instead of having an independent arbiter, um, we have people given time to make a submission, not very much time, especially if they have to get their heads around the whole process.

Um, and they're making a decision, a submission to the government that is pushing this, who is the proxy for the applicant or the applicant itself. And that's just not, not due good due process, not good government and not fair to people. It's also fails to recognize Maori cultural values and the depth and attachment and responsibility that Maori have to look after land.

Um, and for instance, many of us who have land in our Cape, my own personal family, we have a coastal land that has been managed for its ecosystem values. It's a sheep and beef place, but you know, a hot there've been decades of looking after the embark. You've got four minute, got four minutes left.

Well, I think the devil is in the detail. Um, it would so depend on what that, um, committee or whatever is and who appoints them. Um, we've got a, a growing and increasing need for electrification. and, and that sort of thing. So, um, we have, uh, we've got a lot to do over the next, um, 10 to 20 years. Currently we access them via section 1 8 6 of the Resource Management Act. Um, as a, as a network utility operator, uh, we don't, we, because we are not, uh, considered, um, central or local government, we can't access those powers directly. So we, we do have to ask the Minister for Lands to Compulsorily acquire land on our behalf.

Uh, we haven't had, we haven't had to do that a lot in recent memory. The last time we asked to, to access compulsory acquisition powers was back in, in 2014. Um, so we haven't used a lot in the past, but when we do need to use it and if we need to construct a new transmission line and we have dozens, uh, if not hundreds of, um, of easements to acquire or, or property rights to acquire, um, we expect a need to utilize the Public Works Act more going forward with that increasing electrification.

I would just add to that, that we do typically acquire easements as opposed, opposed to a freehold interest in land. Um, so, um, we do sometimes acquire freehold interest, but easements are typically what we acquire for transmission lines. Um, Raven has joined us, so, um, we can probably substitute, um, in here.

Uh, welcome. Sorry, I'm couple minutes Late. We're actually on this. Okay, I'll look at that one. Yeah, I've just, I've just finished a section if you wanted to Jump in. Um, overall, um, we support the policy intent of streamlining the land acquisition process for critical infrastructure, um, and allowing the need support the need for broader, um, public work sector reform, noting that, um, this is the first tranche in that space, uh,

and in particular those changes that were announced in February 22, 25, um, allowing trans power to acquire land under the Public Works Act to streamline processes for building energy infrastructure. Thanks. Yeah, so, um, so the, in terms of the specific, um, changes we've sought to the bill, uh, as I spoke to earlier, trans Power effectively operates the state highway network for electricity.

Um, and currently the, um, the, the, the bill, uh, the list of qualifying projects in the bill is effectively fixed. It includes, um, certain listed, um, fast track projects and it also includes, um, NZ TAs, roads of National Significance Projects.

Um, we, we are, we are seeking that, um, the act, the, the bill, I should say, sorry, be amended to provide for, uh, trans powers major new transmission line build projects and including associated substations, um, to be qualifying projects under the, the, under the, the act.

Um, we suggest, um, that the threshold that there should be a threshold, um, and we suggest that threshold should be, um, projects, um, that are green fields, brand new, um, transmission line builds, um, and with a budget of more than 30 million. Um, and we also suggest in our submission that the process, um, for including those should be through and through through the ordering council process, so that there is, um, the ability for, um, the minister to include those projects as and when they arise by need.

Um, so some examples of those projects, um, we have put in our submission, um, we have a need to supply, uh, Queenstown as a growing, growing city, um, with a need for electricity running out. So we need to, um, build infrastructure to, to meet that growing demand. Similar challenges in the Western Bay of Plenty with TGA and um, also other challenges in the central North Island and also in the Auckland area, south Auckland, and also North Auckland as well.

So we, um, do see a need to utilize the Public Works Act and it would be beneficial for us to be able to utilize, um, the provisions in the bill, um, where we need to, where we need to use those powers. And I'll just add to the comments Matt's made, the projects that we've suggested be included, uh, probably trans powers equivalent to the Rons that NCTA has had, uh, benefiting from the bill.

So we certainly haven't suggested that all trans power projects should benefit from it. It is a sub, it is, it is the big projects that would be, um, of national significance in the same way as salons. Yeah, Thanks. True. Um, so the, the other change that we, um, you've got, You've got four minutes left. Thank thanks Andy. replacement and capacity project. So we had two projects listed in Schedule two of the Fast Tracked Approvals Act, but only one of them seems to have carried through into the, um, the, into the bill. And, um, for reasons that are unclear to us, the, um, our, our high voltage direct current cable replacement project has been left off, um, schedule two A in the bill.

So we are seeking that, that be, that'd be reinstated because we, um, we do see a potential need to utilize the Public Works Act for, for that project. Um, we also just note that, um, we have heard, um, some submissions to this committee, uh, on Friday and earlier today expressing concerns around Maori land.

Um, so we wanted to, to put forward that, um, you know, trans power avoids Maori land wherever possible. We go through a detailed route selection, site selection process when, um, identifying, um, the land that we, we need and, uh, we, we go to every length possible to avoid Maori land. Um, also noting there is, there is case law, um, that Maori land should only be considered as an absolute last resort as well.

Um, anything further to add to that? Okay. I think we can hand over for questions in the time we've got. Okay, thank you, cam. Uh, thank you very much. You, me, made a mention about, um, using easements, uh, mostly instead of public works. How did, how do you come to a figure for compensation around the use of easements?

Yeah, um, yes we do, we do use easements. Um, we, we sometimes do also acquire freehold and, um, and easements can be acquired under the Public Works Act, so it's just a lesser interest in land as freehold. Um, we have those, um, the compensation assessed by a registered valuer and the landowner will have a registered valuer and we will, um, procure services of a, of a registered valuer and we will then negotiate a, um, a settlement amount.

Um, that's how we currently acquire easements. If we had to acquire under the Public Works Act, we would've to work under the compensation methodology under the Public Works Act, which is more regimented. And is there gonna be any kind of, if it was to be adopted into the bill, any kind of, uh, inflation adjusting or any suggestion you have along those lines?

Basically, how did you come up with 30 million? I can answer that question that that is the, um, threshold under the commerce, um, commission regime for trans power for major CapEx projects. So if it's an enhancement or a new line, that is the threshold for going down that route. Cool. Thank you Tangi.

Yeah, thank you Mr. Chair. Um, thank you trans power for your submission.
